title: Slow Cooker Italian Chicken Pasta
subtitle: The kind of dinner that cooks itself.
excerpt: Slow Cooker Italian Chicken Pasta. A slow-cooker dish that does its own work — set it in the morning, serve it at dinner.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook. The bones of the recipe sit in the Italian home-cooking tradition documented by writers like Pellegrino Artusi (La Scienza in Cucina, 1891) and Marcella Hazan.

recipe.batchNotes: A double batch fits a 6-litre slow cooker. Use the second half straight away or freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The slow cooker is the make-ahead.

content.text: Slow Cooker Italian Chicken Pasta. The dish belongs to the Italian home-cooking register: straightforward, ingredient-led, calm at the hob. Adjustable for what you need.
It runs in the slow cooker so the work is at the start of the day and the dinner cooks itself.

items.prepNote: or make your own using basil, oregano and thyme

content.text: Add diced onion, garlic, chicken breasts, cream cheese, marinara sauce, and some seasonings into a cold slow cooker.
Cover and cook on high for 3-4 hours. If your slow cooker runs hot, you might want to check the chicken for doneness at 2 1/2 hours. Also make sure to know how
Once the chicken is cooked, remove, shred and return back to the slow cooker. At this point remove the bay leaves as well. Stir to melt cream cheese into sauce completely.
Cook pasta in a pot on the stove.
Toss with freshly cooked pasta and serve.

TIP - This chicken pasta recipe calls for cream cheese. If left too long in the slow cooker at too high of a heat, it will curdle. The same goes for milk, cream, or greek yoghurt. If you’re worried about curdling because your slow cooker runs hot, try cooking at the low setting for a longer period of time OR stir the dairy in at the end. For this recipe, once you shred the chicken and add it back into the marinara, stir in the cream cheese and melt it into the sauce. It should work just as well!

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong
attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.symptom: Sauce won't cling to the pasta
items.cause: Pasta drained too thoroughly, or sauce too thin.
items.fix: Reserve a ladle of starchy cooking water before draining. Toss it through with the pasta and sauce in the pan over heat — the starch binds everything.

items.cause: Overcooked, or sat in water after draining.
items.fix: Cook a minute less than the packet says, drain, and finish in the sauce. Time it so the pasta meets the sauce in the pan, not the colander.

items.cause: Pan too hot, or cheese added all at once.
items.fix: Lower the heat first. Add the cheese a small handful at a time, stirring continuously, with a splash of pasta water to keep the sauce loose.

items.cause: Tomato or wine hasn't cooked down long enough.
items.fix: Give the sauce another ten to fifteen minutes at a low simmer. A pinch of sugar softens an acidic tomato base.

content.text: Vegetarian: drop the meat and add 200 g mushrooms (chestnut or porcini) sautéed until they squeak.
Lighter sauce: replace cream with crème fraîche stirred in off the heat — same richness, less weight.
The slow cooker is the make-ahead.

The dish sits in the Italian home-cooking tradition: a few good ingredients, a long unhurried cook, and a pan that has fed the family for years. Worth keeping its roots in mind when you taste — the silence between flavours is part of the design.
